Dear Research Team,

            You are collaborating to generate a new research idea based on the following Introduction:

            **Introduction**

             Introduction
Domain shift is a common challenge in many real life applications [16]. For ex-
ample, in medical imaging, models trained on the data from one institution may
not generalize well to another institution with different imaging hardware. Sim-
ilarly, in battery capacity monitoring, models trained on lab-collected data may
perform poorly under diverse operation environments. Obtaining the annotation
for the new domains, however, is often very costly or infeasible. To address this
⋆Hanbing Liu, Jingge Wang, Xuan Zhang, and Yang Li are from the Shenzhen Key
Laboratory of Ubiquitous Data Enabling, SIGS, Tsinghua University.arXiv:2402.16681v2  [cs.LG]  18 Mar 20242 Hanbing Liu et al.
challenge, Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) has been proposed, lever-
aging the labeled data from the source domain to improve the performance of
learning models on the unlabeled target domain [5]. In particular, UDA aims
to align the distributions of the source and target domains using labeled source
data and unlabeled target data, typically by learning domain-invariant represen-
tations [13] or adversarial learning schemes. Nevertheless, one challenge of UDA
is its limited effectiveness when confronted with significant domain shift. Studies
conducted by Zhao et al. [18] have shed light on the relationship between the
domain shift and generalization error in UDA. They have shown that the effec-
tiveness of UDA is bounded by the distributional divergence between the source
and target domain, so the performance of the adapted model on the target do-
main may not be satisfactory with a substantial domain shift. In addressing this
challenge, many works studied the problem of Continuous Domain Adaptation
(CDA) [17].
Instead of directly adapting the model from the source to the target do-
main, CDA captures the underlying domain continuity leveraging a stream of
observed intermediate domains, and gradually bridges the substantial domain
gap by adapting the model progressively. There are various applications of CDA
requiring continuous domains with indexed metadata [6,9]. For example, in med-
ical data analysis, age acts as a continuous metadata for disease diagnosis across
patients of different age groups. In the online battery capacity estimation prob-
lem, the state of health (SoH) of the battery acts as a continuous metadata that
differs across batteries. CDA has attracted a great deal of attention and gained
a rapid performance boost by self-training [7,19], pseudo-labeling [8], adversar-
ial algorithms, optimal transport (OT) [10] and so on. In particular, Oritiz et
al. [10] designed an efficient forward-backward splitting optimization algorithm
for continuous optimal transport (COT) and demonstrated the efficacy of OT in
reducing the domain shift and improving the performance of adaptation models.
While there have been significant advances in CDA, it still faces two critical
challenges, determining the transfer order of intermediate domains in the ab-
sence of continuous metadata and mitigating cumulative errors throughout the
continuous adaptation process. For the first issue, metadata could be missing
or incorrect, and sometimes metadata alone can not fully explain the difference
between data distributions. The proper ordering of intermediate domains is sig-
nificant for CDA in transferring knowledge all the way to the target domain.
Indeed, it is necessary to order the intermediate domains to facilitate continu-
ous transfer without relying on explicit metadata. As a divergence measurement
that takes into account the geometry of the data distributions, Wasserstein dis-
tance (w-distance) [15] plays an important role in deriving the generalization
bound in domain adaptation, which implies

            **Your Task**

            Use only the Introduction above as your literature context. External paper databases, arXiv, author-profile APIs, and web search are not part of this benchmark.

            1. **Context analysis**: Summarize the problem setting, prior approaches mentioned in the Introduction, and implied gaps you can infer from that text alone.

            2. **Brainstorming**: Collaboratively brainstorm potential research ideas that build upon or address gaps in the Introduction.

            3. **Summarization**: Summarize your collective ideas.

            4. **Formulate a New Research Idea**: Develop a new research proposal in the format of the '5q', defined below:

               **Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.**

               **Here are the five core questions:**

               **[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

               Formulate the specific research question you aim to address. Only output one question and do not include any more information.

               **[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

               Explain the broader implications of solving this problem for the research community.
               Discuss how such a paper will affect future research.
               Discuss how addressing this question could advance knowledge or lead to practical applications.

               **[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

               Discuss the challenges and complexities involved in solving this problem.
               Explain why naive or straightforward approaches may fail.
               Identify any technical, theoretical, or practical obstacles that need to be overcome. MAKE IT CLEAR.

               **[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

               Identify gaps or limitations in previous research or existing solutions.
               Discuss any barriers that have prevented this problem from being solved until now.
               Explain how your approach differs from or improves upon prior work. MAKE IT CLEAR.

               **[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

               Outline your proposed methodology in detail, including the method, dataset, and metrics that you plan to use.

**Problem Setting:**
- **Domain Shift:** A significant challenge in real-life applications where models trained on one domain do not generalize well to another due to differences in data distribution.
- **Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA):** Uses labeled source data and unlabeled target data to align domain distributions, often through domain-invariant representations or adversarial learning.
- **Continuous Domain Adaptation (CDA):** Captures domain continuity using intermediate domains to bridge substantial domain gaps progressively.

**Prior Approaches:**
- **UDA:** Leverages labeled source data and unlabeled target data to improve model performance.
- **CDA:** Uses intermediate domains to adapt models progressively, employing techniques like self-training, pseudo-labeling, adversarial algorithms, and optimal transport (OT).

**Implied Gaps:**
- **Transfer Order of Intermediate Domains:** Determining the correct order of intermediate domains without explicit metadata.
- **Cumulative Errors:** Mitigating errors that accumulate during continuous adaptation.

### Brainstorming

1. **Metadata-Free Domain Ordering:**
   - Develop a method to infer the order of intermediate domains without relying on explicit metadata.
   - Use clustering or graph-based approaches to determine the natural progression of domains.

2. **Error Mitigation Techniques:**
   - Introduce mechanisms to detect and correct cumulative errors during continuous adaptation.
   - Explore reinforcement learning to dynamically adjust the adaptation process.

3. **Hybrid Approaches:**
   - Combine CDA with other domain adaptation techniques to improve robustness.
   - Use ensemble methods to aggregate predictions from multiple models adapted to different domains.

4. **Evaluation Metrics:**
   - Develop new evaluation metrics to assess the effectiveness of continuous domain adaptation.
   - Focus on metrics that capture both accuracy and robustness across different domains.

### Summarization

The key challenges in Continuous Domain Adaptation (CDA) are determining the correct order of intermediate domains without metadata and mitigating cumulative errors. Potential solutions include metadata-free domain ordering techniques, error mitigation strategies, hybrid approaches, and new evaluation metrics.

### 1. Introduction

**Domain Shift and Continuous Domain Adaptation (CDA):**
Domain shift is a significant challenge in real-life applications where models trained on one domain do not generalize well to another due to differences in data distribution. Continuous Domain Adaptation (CDA) addresses this issue by capturing domain continuity through intermediate domains, adapting models progressively to reduce domain gaps.

**Research Question:**
How can we determine the transfer order of intermediate domains in CDA without relying on explicit metadata?

**Significance:**
Solving this problem will enhance the effectiveness of CDA by enabling more accurate and efficient domain adaptation. It will allow models to generalize better across diverse domains, which is critical for real-world applications such as medical imaging and battery capacity monitoring. Improved domain adaptation can lead to more robust and reliable models, reducing the need for extensive retraining and annotation efforts.

### 2. Literature Review

**Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) and Continuous Domain Adaptation (CDA):**
UDA techniques include learning domain-invariant representations and adversarial learning schemes. CDA techniques utilize self-training, pseudo-labeling, adversarial algorithms, and optimal transport (OT) to improve adaptation.

**Challenges and Limitations:**
Determining the transfer order of intermediate domains without metadata is complex due to the lack of explicit guidance on domain similarity. Naive approaches may lead to suboptimal adaptation paths, resulting in poor model performance on the target domain. Existing methods often rely on metadata for domain ordering, which is often unavailable or unreliable.

**Gaps in Literature:**
Developing metadata-free methods requires innovative clustering and similarity measures that can accurately capture domain differences. Additionally, existing methods may not effectively mitigate cumulative errors during continuous adaptation, leading to degraded performance over time.

### 3. Methodology

**Proposed Clustering-Based Approach:**
We propose a clustering-based approach to group intermediate domains based on data distribution similarity. This method will utilize Wasserstein distance to measure the geometric differences between domains and develop a hierarchical clustering algorithm to determine the optimal transfer order.

**Wasserstein Distance:**
Wasserstein distance is a metric that measures the geometric differences between probability distributions. It is particularly useful in capturing the underlying data distribution differences between domains.

**Hierarchical Clustering Algorithm:**
The hierarchical clustering algorithm will iteratively merge the closest domains based on Wasserstein distance until a single cluster is formed. The order in which domains are merged will determine the transfer order.

**Datasets:**
We will use synthetic datasets with known domain shifts and real-world datasets with varying data distributions, such as medical imaging datasets and battery capacity monitoring datasets.

### 4. Experimental Setup

**Experimental Design:**
The experimental design will include the selection of datasets, preprocessing steps, and model configurations. We will use a variety of datasets to ensure the robustness of our approach.

**Preprocessing Steps:**
Data preprocessing will involve normalization, augmentation, and feature extraction to ensure consistency across datasets.

**Model Configurations:**
We will use a combination of deep learning models and clustering algorithms to evaluate the proposed method.

**Evaluation Metrics:**
The evaluation metrics will include accuracy, domain adaptation error, computational efficiency, and cumulative error accumulation.

### 5. Results

**Performance Comparisons:**
We will present the results of the experiments, including performance comparisons with existing metadata-free approaches. The results will be illustrated using tables, figures, and graphs.

**Illustrative Findings:**
The proposed method will demonstrate improved domain adaptation performance with reduced cumulative errors and better generalization across diverse domains.

### 6. Discussion

**Interpretation of Results:**
The results will be interpreted in the context of the research questions and objectives. We will discuss the implications of the findings for the field of CDA.

The key findings will be summarized, highlighting the significance of the research and its potential impact on the field.

**Importance of Research:**
The research will enhance the effectiveness of CDA by enabling more accurate and efficient domain adaptation, allowing models to generalize better across diverse domains.
